name: Canopic Jars
description: Canopic jars were essential artifacts in ancient Egyptian funerary practices, serving as receptacles for the deceased's internal organs. These organs, specifically the liver, lungs, intestines, and stomach, were carefully removed during the mummification process and preserved in individual jars. Each jar was typically associated with one of the four sons of Horus: Imsety (human-headed, holding the liver), Hapi (baboon-headed, holding the lungs), Duamutef (jackal-headed, holding the stomach), and Qebehsenuef (falcon-headed, holding the intestines). The jars were placed in the tomb alongside the mummy, intended to ensure the deceased's wholeness and ability to function in the afterlife. The lids of the jars were often sculpted with the likenesses of these protective deities, further emphasizing their role in safeguarding the vital organs. While the heart was considered the seat of intelligence and emotions and was left inside the body, the other organs were vulnerable to decay and therefore required special preservation. The materials used for crafting canopic jars varied, including limestone, alabaster, pottery, and even precious metals, reflecting the wealth and status of the deceased.
subtitle: Ancient Egyptian funerary jars for organ preservation.
origin: Crafted by ancient Egyptian embalmers and artisans as part of the mummification process.
currentLocation: Mostly found in museums and archaeological sites, some lost or destroyed.

keyMyths.title: The Sons of Horus
keyMyths.description: The four sons of Horus, Imsety, Hapi, Duamutef, and Qebehsenuef, were tasked with protecting the internal organs of the deceased. Each son was associated with a specific organ and a cardinal direction, ensuring comprehensive protection in the afterlife. They were seen as essential for the deceased's successful journey.

keyMyths.title: Mummification Rituals
keyMyths.description: The removal and preservation of organs using canopic jars was a crucial part of the mummification process. This elaborate ritual aimed to prepare the deceased for the afterlife by ensuring their physical integrity and providing them with the necessary components for a successful transition.

keyMyths.title: Osiris and the Afterlife
keyMyths.description: The belief in the afterlife and the resurrection of Osiris heavily influenced the funerary practices, including the use of canopic jars. The Egyptians believed that preserving the body and its organs was essential for the deceased to be judged by Osiris and enter the eternal realm.

sources.author: Ikram, Salima
sources.work: Death and Burial in Ancient Egypt
sources.description: Provides a comprehensive overview of Egyptian funerary practices, including the role and significance of canopic jars.
